content:
# Numbers Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 159–160; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 119–120 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_010_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_010_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — FROM THE CLOUD TO THE TRUMPETS: DIVINE GUIDANCE MADE AUDIBLE:** Numbers 10 completes the Sinai legislation and narrates the first departure from the mountain. The chapter divides into three distinct sections of markedly different character. The first (vv. 1–10) contains YHWH's instructions to Moses for making two silver trumpets — instruments of assembly, alarm, and liturgical celebration. The second (vv. 11–28) narrates the actual departure from the wilderness of Sinai in the second year, second month, twentieth day, providing the precise marching order of the tribes under their standards. The third (vv. 29–36) records Moses' exchange with Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite and concludes with the two brief poetic utterances spoken when the Ark set out and when it rested — verses bracketed in the Masoretic tradition with inverted nuns (׆), marking them as a distinct literary unit. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 10 begins on page 159 (continuing from the cloud-narrative of chapter 9) and extends onto page 160. The Vaticanus LXX preserves the chapter across pages 119–120 (pa_0119 to pa_0120), following the MT closely with some notable divergences in the tribal order. The parashah marker ס (closed section) appears between v. 10 and v. 11, separating the trumpet legislation from the departure narrative.

---

## The Silver Trumpets (vv. 1–10)

**1** And-spoke [va-yedabber] YHWH unto [el] Moses [Mosheh], saying [le'mor]:

**2** "Make [aseh] for-yourself [lekha] two [shtei] trumpets of [khatsotsrot] silver [kesef]; of-hammered-work [miqshah] you-shall-make [ta'aseh] [et]-them [otam]; and-they-shall-be [ve-hayu] for-you [lekha] for-the-summoning of [le-miqra] the-congregation [ha-edah] and-for-the-setting-out of [u-le-massa] the-camps [ha-makhanot].

> **Key terms:** חֲצֹצְרֹת (khatsotsrot) = "trumpets" — distinct from the שׁוֹפָר (shofar, "ram's horn"). These are straight, metallic instruments made of beaten silver. מִקְשָׁה (miqshah) = "hammered work" — the same construction technique used for the golden menorah (Exod 25:18, 31, 36). The trumpets are formed from a single piece of beaten silver, not cast or assembled. לְמִקְרָא הָעֵדָה (le-miqra ha-edah) = "for the summoning of the congregation" — from קרא (qara', "to call/summon"). וּלְמַסַּע הַמַּחֲנוֹת (u-le-massa ha-makhanot) = "and for the setting out of the camps" — from נסע (nasa', "to journey/pull up stakes"). The two functions — assembly and marching — correspond to the two basic modes of Israel's wilderness existence: encamped and journeying. **LXX:** ποίησον σεαυτῷ δύο σάλπιγγας ἀργυρᾶς, ἐλατὰς ποιήσεις αὐτάς ("make for yourself two trumpets of silver, beaten you shall make them") — rendering מִקְשָׁה as ἐλατάς ("beaten/hammered").

**3** And-they-shall-blow [ve-taqe'u] on-them [ba-hennah], and-shall-assemble [ve-no'adu] unto-you [elekha] all [kol] the-congregation [ha-edah] unto [el] the-entrance of [petakh] the-Tent of [Ohel] Meeting [Mo'ed].

> **Key terms:** תָּקְעוּ (taqe'u) = "they shall blow" — from תקע (taqa', "to thrust/blow/sound"). When both trumpets are blown together, the entire congregation gathers. The location of assembly — פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (petakh Ohel Mo'ed, "the entrance of the Tent of Meeting") — is the standard gathering place before the sanctuary (cf. Lev 1:3; 8:3; Num 6:18). **LXX:** καὶ σαλπιεῖς ἐν αὐταῖς, καὶ συναχθήσεται πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγὴ ἐπὶ τὴν θύραν τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("and you shall sound them, and all the assembly shall be gathered at the door of the tent of testimony").

**4** And-if [ve-im] on-one [be-akhat] they-blow [yitqa'u], and-shall-assemble [ve-no'adu] unto-you [elekha] the-leaders [ha-nesi'im], the-heads of [rashei] the-thousands of [alfei] Israel [Yisra'el].

> **Key terms:** בְּאַחַת (be-akhat) = "on one" — i.e., when only one trumpet is blown. This creates a graded signaling system: two trumpets for the whole assembly, one trumpet for the leaders only. הַנְּשִׂיאִים (ha-nesi'im) = "the leaders/princes" — the same tribal heads enumerated in Numbers 1:5–16 and 7:1–83. רָאשֵׁי אַלְפֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (rashei alfei Yisra'el) = "the heads of the thousands of Israel" — אֶלֶף (elef) may mean literally "a thousand" or may refer to a military/clan unit. **LXX:** καὶ ἐὰν ἐν μιᾷ σαλπίσωσιν, προσελεύσονται πρὸς σὲ πάντες οἱ ἄρχοντες, ἀρχηγοὶ χιλιάδων Ισραηλ ("and if they sound on one, all the rulers shall come to you, the chiefs of [the] thousands of Israel").

**5** And-you-shall-blow [u-teqa'tem] an-alarm [teru'ah], and-shall-journey [ve-nas'u] the-camps [ha-makhanot] the-ones-encamping [ha-khonim] eastward [qedmah].

> **Key terms:** תְּרוּעָה (teru'ah) = "alarm/blast" — from רוע (rua', "to shout/sound an alarm"). This is a distinct sound from the plain blowing (תְּקִיעָה, teqi'ah) of vv. 3–4. The teru'ah is a broken, staccato blast signaling departure, while the teqi'ah is a long, sustained blast signaling assembly. קֵדְמָה (qedmah) = "eastward" — the camps on the east side (Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, per Num 2:3–9) depart first at the first alarm. **LXX:** καὶ σαλπιεῖτε σημασίαν, καὶ ἐξαροῦσιν αἱ παρεμβολαὶ αἱ παρεμβάλλουσαι ἀνατολάς ("and you shall sound a signal, and the camps camping toward the east shall set out").

**6** And-you-shall-blow [u-teqa'tem] an-alarm [teru'ah] a-second-time [shenit], and-shall-journey [ve-nas'u] the-camps [ha-makhanot] the-ones-encamping [ha-khonim] southward [teimanah]; an-alarm [teru'ah] they-shall-blow [yitqe'u] for-their-journeyings [le-mas'eihem].

> **Key terms:** תֵּימָנָה (teimanah) = "southward" — the camps on the south side (Reuben, Simeon, Gad, per Num 2:10–16) depart at the second alarm. The MT mentions only east and south explicitly; the pattern implies that the third and fourth alarms set the west and north camps in motion, respectively. לְמַסְעֵיהֶם (le-mas'eihem) = "for their journeyings" — a general statement that the alarm-blast accompanies each stage of departure. **LXX:** καὶ σαλπιεῖτε σημασίαν δευτέραν, καὶ ἐξαροῦσιν αἱ παρεμβολαὶ αἱ παρεμβάλλουσαι λίβα ("and you shall sound a second signal, and the camps camping toward the south shall set out"). The Vaticanus adds that a third signal sets out the western camps and a fourth the northern — an expansion not found in the MT.

**7** And-in-the-assembling [u-ve-haqhil] [et] the-assembly [ha-qahal], you-shall-blow [titqe'u] and-not [ve-lo] you-shall-sound-an-alarm [tari'u].

> **Key terms:** The distinction between תָּקַע (taqa', "to blow a sustained blast") and הֵרִיעַ (heri'a, "to sound an alarm/broken blast") is critical. For assembly, a plain sustained blast (teqi'ah); for marching, a staccato alarm (teru'ah). This distinction survived into later Jewish liturgy, where the shofar service on Rosh Hashanah preserves three types of blasts: teqi'ah (long), shevarim (three medium), and teru'ah (nine short). **LXX:** καὶ ὅταν συναγάγητε τὴν συναγωγήν, σαλπιεῖτε καὶ οὐ σημασίᾳ ("and whenever you gather the assembly, you shall sound and not with a signal").

**8** And-the-sons of [u-venei] Aaron [Aharon], the-priests [ha-kohanim], they-shall-blow [yitqe'u] on-the-trumpets [ba-khatsotsrot]; and-they-shall-be [ve-hayu] for-you [lakhem] for-a-statute of [le-khuqqat] perpetuity [olam] for-your-generations [le-doroteikhem].

> **Key terms:** The exclusive priestly prerogative over the trumpets is established here: only the sons of Aaron may sound them. This distinguishes the silver trumpets from the shofar, which could be blown by non-priests. לְחֻקַּת עוֹלָם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם (le-khuqqat olam le-doroteikhem) = "for a statute of perpetuity for your generations" — this ordinance is not temporary wilderness legislation but a permanent institution. The Chronicler records priestly trumpeters in the Temple (2 Chr 5:12–13; 29:26–28), demonstrating continuity of this statute. **LXX:** καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ Ααρων οἱ ἱερεῖς σαλπιοῦσιν ταῖς σάλπιγξιν, καὶ ἔσται ὑμῖν νόμιμον αἰώνιον εἰς τὰς γενεὰς ὑμῶν ("and the sons of Aaron the priests shall sound the trumpets, and it shall be for you an eternal ordinance for your generations").

**9** And-when [ve-khi] you-shall-go [tavo'u] to-war [milkhamah] in-your-land [be-artsekhem] against [al] the-adversary [ha-tsar] the-one-oppressing [ha-tsorer] [et]-you [etkhem], and-you-shall-sound-an-alarm [va-hare'otem] on-the-trumpets [ba-khatsotsrot], and-you-shall-be-remembered [ve-nizkartem] before [lifnei] YHWH your-God [Eloheikhem], and-you-shall-be-saved [ve-nosha'tem] from [me] your-enemies [oyeveikhem].

> **Key terms:** הַצַּר הַצֹּרֵר (ha-tsar ha-tsorer) = "the adversary the-one-oppressing" — an emphatic doubling using the noun צַר (tsar, "adversary/distress") and the active participle צֹרֵר (tsorer, "one who oppresses/afflicts"), both from the root צרר (tsarar). וְנִזְכַּרְתֶּם (ve-nizkartem) = "and you shall be remembered" — the niphal of זכר (zakhar, "to remember"). The trumpet-blast serves as a memorial before YHWH, prompting divine intervention. This is not magical causation but covenantal petition: the trumpet-sound brings Israel's distress before YHWH, who responds with deliverance. **LXX:** καὶ ἐὰν ἐξέλθητε εἰς πόλεμον ἐν τῇ γῇ ὑμῶν πρὸς τοὺς ὑπεναντίους τοὺς ἀνθεστηκότας ὑμῖν, καὶ σημανεῖτε ταῖς σάλπιγξιν, καὶ ἀναμνησθήσεσθε ἔναντι κυρίου καὶ διασωθήσεσθε ἀπὸ τῶν ἐχθρῶν ὑμῶν ("and if you go out to war in your land against the opponents who resist you, you shall signal with the trumpets, and you shall be remembered before [the] Lord, and you shall be saved from your enemies").

**10** And-in-the-day of [u-ve-yom] your-gladness [simkhatkhem] and-in-your-appointed-feasts [u-ve-mo'adeikhem] and-in-the-beginnings of [u-ve-rashei] your-months [khodsheikhem], and-you-shall-blow [u-teqa'tem] on-the-trumpets [ba-khatsotsrot] over [al] your-burnt-offerings [oloteikhem] and-over [ve-al] the-sacrifices of [zivkhei] your-peace-offerings [shalmeikhem]; and-they-shall-be [ve-hayu] for-you [lakhem] for-a-memorial [le-zikkaron] before [lifnei] your-God [Eloheikhem]; I [ani] [am] YHWH your-God [Eloheikhem]."

> **Key terms:** The three liturgical occasions: (1) שִׂמְחָה (simkhah, "gladness") — joyful celebrations; (2) מוֹעֲדִים (mo'adim, "appointed feasts") — the pilgrimage festivals (Passover, Weeks, Tabernacles); (3) רָאשֵׁי חָדְשֵׁיכֶם (rashei khodsheikhem, "beginnings of your months") — the new moon observances. לְזִכָּרוֹן (le-zikkaron) = "for a memorial/remembrance" — the trumpets function as a זִכָּרוֹן before God, paralleling the memorial stones (Josh 4:7) and the memorial portion of the grain offering (Lev 2:2). The closing formula אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם (ani YHWH Eloheikhem, "I [am] YHWH your God") seals the legislation with divine self-identification — the same formula that opens the Decalogue (Exod 20:2). **LXX:** καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις τῆς εὐφροσύνης ὑμῶν καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἑορταῖς ὑμῶν καὶ ἐν ταῖς νεομηνίαις ὑμῶν ... καὶ ἔσται ὑμῖν ἀνάμνησις ἔναντι τοῦ θεοῦ ὑμῶν· ἐγὼ κύριος ὁ θεὸς ὑμῶν ("and in the days of your gladness and in your feasts and in your new moons ... and it shall be for you a memorial before your God; I [am the] Lord your God").

---

## The Departure from Sinai (vv. 11–28)

**11** And-it-was [va-yehi] in-the-year [ba-shanah] the-second [ha-shenit], in-the-month [ba-khodesh] the-second [ha-sheni], on-the-twentieth [be-esrim] in-the-month [ba-khodesh], went-up [na'alah] the-cloud [he-anan] from-upon [me-al] the-Tabernacle of [Mishkan] the-Testimony [ha-Edut].

> **Key terms:** The date is precise: the twentieth day of the second month (Iyyar) of the second year after the Exodus. The census was taken on the first of this month (Num 1:1); the second Passover on the fourteenth (Num 9:11); and now, six days later, the cloud lifts. מִשְׁכַּן הָעֵדֻת (Mishkan ha-Edut) = "the Tabernacle of the Testimony" — combining the dwelling-language (מִשְׁכָּן) with the covenant-testimony language (עֵדוּת), as in 9:15. The lifting of the cloud — the signal described at length in 9:15–23 — now triggers the first actual departure. **LXX:** ἀνέβη ἡ νεφέλη ἀπὸ τῆς σκηνῆς τοῦ μαρτυρίου ("the cloud went up from the tent of the testimony").

**12** And-journeyed [va-yis'u] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] for-their-journeyings [le-mas'eihem] from-the-wilderness of [mi-midbar] Sinai [Sinai]; and-dwelt [va-yishkon] the-cloud [he-anan] in-the-wilderness of [be-midbar] Paran [Pa'ran].

> **Key terms:** לְמַסְעֵיהֶם (le-mas'eihem) = "for their journeyings" — i.e., according to their prescribed marching order. מִדְבַּר פָּארָן (midbar Pa'ran) = "the wilderness of Paran" — a large desert region in the northeastern Sinai Peninsula, south of Canaan. This is the next major station after Sinai, though Numbers 33:16 records intermediate stops (Kibroth-hattaavah and Hazeroth). The cloud's settling in Paran anticipates the spy narrative of Numbers 13:3, 26. **LXX:** καὶ κατέπαυσεν ἡ νεφέλη ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ τοῦ Φαραν ("and the cloud rested in the wilderness of Pharan").

**13** And-they-journeyed [va-yis'u] at-the-first [ba-rishonah] upon [al] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH by-the-hand of [be-yad] Moses [Mosheh].

> **Key terms:** בָּרִאשֹׁנָה (ba-rishonah) = "at the first [time]" — this was the first journey under the trumpet-and-cloud system just legislated. עַל פִּי יְהוָה בְּיַד מֹשֶׁה (al pi YHWH be-yad Mosheh) = "upon the mouth of YHWH by the hand of Moses" — the same phrase that closed the cloud-narrative in 9:23, creating continuity between the legislation and its first execution. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξῆραν πρῶτοι διὰ φωνῆς κυρίου ἐν χειρὶ Μωυσῆ ("and they set out first through [the] voice of [the] Lord by [the] hand of Moses").

**14** And-journeyed [ve-yissa] the-standard of [degel] the-camp of [makhaneh] the-sons of [benei] Judah [Yehudah] at-the-first [ba-rishonah] by-their-hosts [le-tsiv'otam]; and-over [ve-al] its-host [tseva'o] [was] Nahshon [Nakhshon] son of [ben] Amminadab [Amminadav].

> **Key terms:** דֶּגֶל (degel) = "standard/banner" — the divisional flag under which three tribes march together (cf. Num 2:3). Judah leads the entire march, as prescribed in Numbers 2:9. נַחְשׁוֹן בֶּן עַמִּינָדָב (Nakhshon ben Amminadav) = "Nahshon son of Amminadab" — Judah's tribal leader, also listed in Num 1:7; 2:3; 7:12. Nahshon is an ancestor of David (Ruth 4:20) and appears in both Matthean and Lukan genealogies of Jesus (Matt 1:4; Luke 3:32). **LXX:** καὶ ἐξῆρεν τάγμα παρεμβολῆς υἱῶν Ιουδα πρῶτοι σὺν δυνάμει αὐτῶν, καὶ ἐπὶ τῆς δυνάμεως αὐτῶν Ναασσων υἱὸς Αμιναδαβ ("and the division of the camp of the sons of Judah set out first with their force, and over their force [was] Naassōn son of Aminadab").

**15** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Issachar [Yissakhar] [was] Nethanel [Netan'el] son of [ben] Zuar [Tsu'ar].

**16** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Zebulun [Zevulun] [was] Eliab [Eli'av] son of [ben] Helon [Khelon].

> **Key terms (vv. 15–16):** The three tribes under Judah's standard — Judah, Issachar, Zebulun — march as the first division, matching the arrangement of Numbers 2:3–9. The leaders are identical to those named in the census (Num 1:8–9) and the offering sequence (Num 7:18, 24).

**17** And-was-taken-down [ve-hurad] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan], and-journeyed [ve-nas'u] the-sons of [benei] Gershon [Gershon] and-the-sons of [u-venei] Merari [Merari], bearers of [nos'ei] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan].

> **Key terms:** הוּרַד (hurad) = "was taken down" — the hophal of ירד (yarad, "to descend/take down"). The Tabernacle is disassembled between the first and second divisions so that it can be re-erected before the Kohathites arrive with the holy vessels (v. 21). The Gershonites carried the curtains and coverings (Num 4:24–26); the Merarites carried the frames, bars, pillars, and bases (Num 4:31–33). **LXX:** καὶ καθαιρεθήσεται ἡ σκηνή, καὶ ἐξαροῦσιν οἱ υἱοὶ Γεδσων καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ Μεραρι αἴροντες τὴν σκηνήν ("and the tent shall be taken down, and the sons of Gedson and the sons of Merari shall set out, carrying the tent").

**18** And-journeyed [ve-nassa] the-standard of [degel] the-camp of [makhaneh] Reuben [Re'uven] by-their-hosts [le-tsiv'otam]; and-over [ve-al] its-host [tseva'o] [was] Elizur [Elitsur] son of [ben] Shedeur [Shedei'ur].

**19** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Simeon [Shim'on] [was] Shelumiel [Shelumi'el] son of [ben] Zurishaddai [Tsuri-Shaddai].

**20** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Gad [Gad] [was] Eliasaph [Elyasaf] son of [ben] Deuel [De'u'el].

> **Key terms (vv. 18–20):** The second division — Reuben, Simeon, Gad — under Reuben's standard, as prescribed in Numbers 2:10–16. דְּעוּאֵל (De'u'el) = the Leningrad Codex consistently reads דְּעוּאֵל here and in 1:14; 7:42, 47, while Num 2:14 has the variant רְעוּאֵל (Re'u'el). The ד/ר confusion is one of the most common in Hebrew manuscript transmission due to the near-identical form of the two letters.

**21** And-journeyed [ve-nas'u] the-Kohathites [ha-Qehatim], bearers of [nos'ei] the-sanctuary [ha-miqdash]; and-they-set-up [ve-heqimu] [et] the-Tabernacle [ha-Mishkan] until [ad] their-coming [bo'am].

> **Key terms:** הַמִּקְדָּשׁ (ha-miqdash) = "the sanctuary" — referring to the holy vessels (the Ark, the table, the lampstand, the altars) which the Kohathites carried on their shoulders (Num 4:4–15). The Gershonites and Merarites (v. 17) traveled ahead with the structural components so that the Tabernacle would be erected (וְהֵקִימוּ, ve-heqimu, "and they set up") before the Kohathites arrived with the sacred objects. This logistical arrangement ensures that the holy items are never left without their housing. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξαροῦσιν οἱ υἱοὶ Κααθ αἴροντες τὰ ἅγια, καὶ στήσουσιν τὴν σκηνὴν ἕως παραγένωνται ("and the sons of Kaath shall set out carrying the holy things, and they shall set up the tent before they arrive").

**22** And-journeyed [ve-nassa] the-standard of [degel] the-camp of [makhaneh] the-sons of [benei] Ephraim [Efrayim] by-their-hosts [le-tsiv'otam]; and-over [ve-al] its-host [tseva'o] [was] Elishama [Elishama] son of [ben] Ammihud [Ammihud].

**23** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Manasseh [Menasheh] [was] Gamaliel [Gamli'el] son of [ben] Pedahzur [Pedahtsur].

**24** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Benjamin [Binyamin] [was] Abidan [Avidan] son of [ben] Gideoni [Gid'oni].

> **Key terms (vv. 22–24):** The third division — Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin — under Ephraim's standard (Num 2:18–24). These are the three tribes descended from Rachel (Ephraim and Manasseh through Joseph; Benjamin directly). Their position in the middle-rear of the march places them between the Levitical transport and the final rearguard.

**25** And-journeyed [ve-nassa] the-standard of [degel] the-camp of [makhaneh] the-sons of [benei] Dan [Dan] by-their-hosts [le-tsiv'otam], the-rearguard [me'assef] of-all [le-khol] the-camps [ha-makhanot]; and-over [ve-al] its-host [tseva'o] [was] Ahiezer [Akhi'ezer] son of [ben] Ammishaddai [Ammi-Shaddai].

> **Key terms:** מְאַסֵּף (me'assef) = "the rearguard/gatherer" — from אסף (asaf, "to gather"). Dan's division serves as the rearguard, gathering up stragglers and protecting the rear of the march. This military function reflects Dan's position as the last and northernmost camp (Num 2:25). The same term is used of Dan's rearguard role in the Judges period (Judg 18). **LXX:** ἐξαροῦσιν τάγμα παρεμβολῆς υἱῶν Δαν ἔσχατοι πασῶν τῶν παρεμβολῶν ("the division of the camp of the sons of Dan shall set out last of all the camps").

**26** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Asher [Asher] [was] Pagiel [Pag'i'el] son of [ben] Ochran [Okhran].

**27** And-over [ve-al] the-host of [tseva] the-tribe of [matteh] the-sons of [benei] Naphtali [Naftali] [was] Ahira [Akhi'ra] son of [ben] Enan [Enan].

**28** These [eleh] [were] the-journeyings of [mas'ei] the-Sons of [benei] Israel [Yisra'el] by-their-hosts [le-tsiv'otam]; and-they-journeyed [va-yissa'u].

> **Key terms (vv. 26–28):** Dan, Asher, Naphtali — the fourth and final division, completing the march. The concluding formula אֵלֶּה מַסְעֵי בְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לְצִבְאֹתָם וַיִּסָּעוּ ("these [were] the journeyings of the Sons of Israel by their hosts, and they journeyed") mirrors the opening of the departure narrative, forming an inclusio around the marching-order section (vv. 14–28). **LXX:** αὗται αἱ στρατιαὶ υἱῶν Ισραηλ, καὶ ἐξῆραν σὺν δυνάμει αὐτῶν ("these [were] the armies of the Sons of Israel, and they set out with their force").

---

## Moses and Hobab; The Ark's Setting Out (vv. 29–36)

**29** And-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh] to-Hobab [le-Khovav] son of [ben] Reuel [Re'u'el] the-Midianite [ha-Midyani], father-in-law of [khoten] Moses [Mosheh]: "Journeying [nos'im] we [anakhnu] unto [el] the-place [ha-maqom] which [asher] YHWH said [amar], '[Et]-it [oto] I-shall-give [etten] to-you [lakhem].' Come [lekhah] with-us [ittanu] and-we-shall-do-good [ve-hetavnu] to-you [lakh], for [ki] YHWH has-spoken [dibber] good [tov] concerning [al] Israel [Yisra'el]."

> **Key terms:** חֹבָב בֶּן רְעוּאֵל הַמִּדְיָנִי (Khovav ben Re'u'el ha-Midyani) = "Hobab son of Reuel the Midianite." The identification of Moses' father-in-law is one of the Torah's longstanding puzzles. Exodus 2:18 names Reuel; Exodus 3:1 and 18:1 name Jethro (Yitro); Judges 4:11 calls Hobab the father-in-law of Moses. The simplest reading here is that Reuel is the father, Hobab is his son (Moses' brother-in-law), and חֹתֵן (khoten) can mean "in-law" more broadly — though the traditional rendering "father-in-law" remains defensible. לְכָה (lekhah) = "come!" — an archaic/emphatic form of the imperative לֵךְ (lekh), with paragogic ה. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς τῷ Ιωβαβ υἱῷ Ραγουηλ τῷ Μαδιανίτῃ τῷ γαμβρῷ Μωυσῆ ("and Moses said to Iobab son of Ragouēl the Midianite, the kinsman-by-marriage of Moses") — the LXX renders the name as Ιωβαβ (Iobab) and uses γαμβρός, which can mean "son-in-law, brother-in-law, or father-in-law."

**30** And-he-said [va-yomer] unto-him [elav]: "Not [lo] I-shall-go [elekh]; but [ki im] unto [el] my-land [artsi] and-unto [ve-el] my-kindred [moladeti] I-shall-go [elekh]."

> **Key terms:** מוֹלַדְתִּי (moladeti) = "my kindred/birthplace" — from ילד (yalad, "to bear/beget"). Hobab declines Moses' invitation, preferring to return to Midian. This echoes (in reverse) Abraham's departure from his kindred (Gen 12:1). **LXX:** οὐ πορεύσομαι, ἀλλ᾽ εἰς τὴν γῆν μου καὶ εἰς τὴν γενεάν μου ("I shall not go, but [rather] to my land and to my family").

**31** And-he-said [va-yomer]: "Please [na], do-not [al] forsake [ta'azov] [et]-us [otanu], because [ki] on-account-of [al ken] you-know [yada'ta] our-encamping [khanotenu] in-the-wilderness [ba-midbar], and-you-shall-be [ve-hayita] for-us [lanu] for-eyes [le-einayim].

> **Key terms:** לְעֵינָיִם (le-einayim) = "for eyes" — i.e., as a guide who sees the way through the wilderness. Moses values Hobab's knowledge of the desert terrain. This verse presents a theological tension: if the cloud guides Israel, why does Moses need a human guide? The answer may be that the cloud indicates the general direction and timing of travel, while a desert-wise guide helps navigate the specific terrain — wadis, water sources, and safe passages. עַל כֵּן (al ken) = "on account of [this]" — here meaning "because/since." **LXX:** μὴ καταλίπῃς ἡμᾶς, οὗ εἵνεκεν ἦσθα μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ καὶ ἔσῃ ἐν ἡμῖν πρεσβύτης ("do not leave us, since you were with us in the wilderness and you shall be an elder among us") — the LXX renders לְעֵינָיִם as πρεσβύτης ("elder"), an interpretive rather than literal rendering.

**32** And-it-shall-be [ve-hayah], when [ki] you-shall-go [telekh] with-us [immanu], and-it-shall-be [ve-hayah] — the-good [ha-tov] the-that [ha-hu] which [asher] YHWH shall-do-good [yeitiv] with-us [immanu], and-we-shall-do-good [ve-hetavnu] to-you [lakh]."

> **Key terms:** Moses promises that whatever good YHWH grants to Israel, Hobab will share in it. The verb הֵיטִיב (heitiv, "to do good/deal well") appears three times in vv. 29–32 (ve-hetavnu, yeitiv, ve-hetavnu), binding Moses' promises to YHWH's. The text does not record Hobab's final answer, but Judges 1:16 and 4:11 indicate that the Kenites (descendants of Moses' father-in-law) did settle in Canaan, suggesting he accepted. **LXX:** καὶ ἔσται ἐὰν πορευθῇς μεθ᾽ ἡμῶν ... καὶ εὖ σε ποιήσομεν ("and it shall be, if you go with us ... we shall do well to you").

**33** And-they-journeyed [va-yis'u] from-the-mountain of [me-har] YHWH [a] journey of [derekh] three [sheloshet] days [yamim]; and-the-Ark of [va-Aron] the-Covenant of [berit] YHWH [was] journeying [nose'a] before-them [lifneihem] [a] journey of [derekh] three [sheloshet] days [yamim] to-seek-out [la-tur] for-them [lahem] a-resting-place [menukhah].

> **Key terms:** הַר יְהוָה (har YHWH) = "the mountain of YHWH" — Sinai is here called by this exalted title, the only occurrence of this phrase for Sinai in the Torah (though cf. "the mountain of God" in Exod 3:1; 4:27; 18:5; 24:13). דֶּרֶךְ שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים (derekh sheloshet yamim) = "a journey of three days" — a standard unit of travel distance (cf. Gen 30:36; Exod 3:18; 5:3; 8:23). לָתוּר (la-tur) = "to seek out/spy out" — the same verb (תור, tur) used for the spies' mission in Numbers 13:2, 16–17. The Ark goes ahead as a divine scout, seeking a place for Israel to rest. מְנוּחָה (menukhah) = "resting place" — from נוח (nuakh, "to rest"). **LXX:** καὶ ἀπῆραν ἐκ τοῦ ὄρους κυρίου ὁδὸν τριῶν ἡμερῶν, καὶ ἡ κιβωτὸς τῆς διαθήκης κυρίου προεπορεύετο πρότερα αὐτῶν ὁδὸν τριῶν ἡμερῶν κατασκέψασθαι αὐτοῖς ἀνάπαυσιν ("and they set out from the mountain of [the] Lord a journey of three days, and the ark of the covenant of [the] Lord went before them a journey of three days, to spy out for them a resting place").

**34** And-the-cloud of [va-anan] YHWH [was] upon-them [aleihem] by-day [yomam] in-their-journeying [be-nos'am] from [min] the-camp [ha-makhaneh].

> **Key terms:** The cloud remains over Israel during the daytime march — a continuation of the protective and guiding presence described in 9:15–23. This verse connects the cloud-theology of chapter 9 with the actual journey. **LXX:** καὶ ἡ νεφέλη κυρίου ἦν σκιάζουσα ἐπ᾽ αὐτοῖς ἡμέρας ἐν τῷ ἐξαίρειν αὐτοὺς ἐκ τῆς παρεμβολῆς ("and the cloud of [the] Lord was overshadowing them by day in their setting out from the camp") — the LXX adds σκιάζουσα ("overshadowing/shading"), interpreting the cloud's function as providing shade from the desert sun.

**35** And-it-was [va-yehi] in-the-journeying of [bi-nso'a] the-Ark [ha-Aron], and-said [va-yomer] Moses [Mosheh]: "Arise [qumah], YHWH, and-let-be-scattered [ve-yafutsu] your-enemies [oyevekha], and-let-flee [ve-yanusu] the-ones-hating-you [mesan'ekha] from-before-you [mi-panekha]."

> **Key terms:** קוּמָה יְהוָה (qumah YHWH) = "Arise, YHWH!" — this battle-cry invokes YHWH as a divine warrior who rises to scatter his enemies. The language is paralleled in Psalm 68:2 [Heb. 68:1] — יָקוּם אֱלֹהִים יָפוּצוּ אוֹיְבָיו ("let God arise, let his enemies be scattered") — which may be a liturgical expansion of this ancient formula. מְשַׂנְאֶיךָ (mesan'ekha) = "the ones hating you" — the piel participle of שׂנא (sane', "to hate"), indicating active, persistent hostility. In the Leningrad Codex, this verse and the next (v. 36) are bracketed by inverted nuns (׆ ... ׆), marking them as a distinct textual unit. The Talmud (Shabbat 115b–116a) debated the significance of these marks, with one opinion holding that these two verses constitute a separate "book," making the Torah effectively seven books rather than five (Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers before v. 35, the two-verse "book," Numbers after v. 36, Deuteronomy). **LXX:** καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ ἐξαίρειν τὴν κιβωτόν, καὶ εἶπεν Μωυσῆς· ἐξεγέρθητι, κύριε, καὶ διασκορπισθήτωσαν οἱ ἐχθροί σου, καὶ φυγέτωσαν πάντες οἱ μισοῦντές σε ("and it was in the setting out of the Ark, and Moses said: 'Arise, Lord, and let your enemies be scattered, and let all who hate you flee'") — the LXX adds πάντες ("all") before "who hate you," an intensification not present in the MT.

**36** And-in-its-resting [u-ve-nukhoh], he-would-say [yomar]: "Return [shuvah], YHWH, [to] the-myriads [rivevot] of the-thousands of [alfei] Israel [Yisra'el]."

> **Key terms:** שׁוּבָה יְהוָה רִבְבוֹת אַלְפֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (shuvah YHWH rivevot alfei Yisra'el) = "Return, YHWH, [to] the myriads of the thousands of Israel." When the Ark rests, Moses calls YHWH back to dwell among the vast multitudes of Israel. רִבְבוֹת (rivevot) = "myriads/ten-thousands" and אַלְפֵי (alfei) = "thousands" — together conveying the immense host of Israel. The two utterances (vv. 35–36) form a pair: YHWH rises as warrior when the Ark moves and returns as indwelling presence when the Ark rests. וּבְנֻחֹה (u-ve-nukhoh) = "and in its resting" — from נוח (nuakh, "to rest"), the same root as מְנוּחָה (menukhah, "resting place") in v. 33. The inverted nuns that bracket vv. 35–36 in the Masoretic tradition are visible in the Leningrad Codex. **LXX:** καὶ ἐν τῇ καταπαύσει εἶπεν· ἐπίστρεφε, κύριε, χιλιάδας μυριάδας ἐν τῷ Ισραηλ ("and in the resting he said: 'Return, Lord, [to the] thousands [of] myriads in Israel") — the LXX reverses the order to χιλιάδας μυριάδας ("thousands of myriads") compared to the MT's רִבְבוֹת אַלְפֵי ("myriads of thousands").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 159–160)

1. **Chapter boundary and parashah structure:** Numbers 10 begins on page 159 of the Leningrad Codex, following the conclusion of the cloud-narrative (9:15–23). A closed parashah marker (ס) appears between v. 10 and v. 11, separating the trumpet legislation (vv. 1–10) from the departure narrative (vv. 11–28). The text continues onto page 160, where the Hobab dialogue and Ark utterances conclude the chapter.

2. **The inverted nuns (נוני"ן הפוכין) at vv. 35–36:** The most distinctive scribal feature in this chapter is the pair of inverted nun marks (׆) that bracket verses 35–36 in the Leningrad Codex. These marks appear before v. 35 and after v. 36, visually isolating these two verses from the surrounding text. The Talmud (Shabbat 115b–116a) records that Rabbi [Yehudah ha-Nasi] placed marks around these verses because they are "a book unto themselves," and that according to this view the Torah contains seven books rather than five: (1) Genesis, (2) Exodus, (3) Leviticus, (4) Numbers 1:1–10:34, (5) Numbers 10:35–36, (6) Numbers 11:1–36:13, (7) Deuteronomy. The number seven is derived from Proverbs 9:1 — "Wisdom has built her house; she has hewn her seven pillars." Other opinions hold the marks indicate that these verses are not in their original position and belong elsewhere in the Torah. The inverted nuns are clearly visible in the Leningrad Codex on page 160.

3. **Marching-order section (vv. 14–28):** The tribal marching order matches Numbers 2 exactly — Judah's division first (east), then Reuben (south), then Ephraim (west), then Dan (north). All twelve tribal leaders named here are identical to those in the census lists of chapters 1–2 and the offering sequence of chapter 7. The scribal hand shows no corrections or erasures in this repetitive section.

4. **Paragraph spacing:** Small spacing within the column text marks verse-group divisions, particularly between the trumpet legislation (vv. 1–10), the date of departure (v. 11), and the marching order proper (vv. 14–28). A further spacing appears before v. 29 (the Hobab narrative), indicating a recognized narrative shift.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pp. 119–120)

5. **LXX expansion in v. 6:** The Vaticanus text at v. 6 contains an expansion not found in the MT. After mentioning the second alarm for the southern camps, the LXX adds instructions for third and fourth alarms to set out the western and northern camps, respectively. The MT mentions only two alarms explicitly (east and south) and then states generally that alarms are blown "for their journeyings." The LXX completes the logical pattern that the MT leaves implicit. This is either a case of LXX harmonization or reflects a Vorlage that contained the fuller text.

6. **LXX addition of πάντες in v. 35:** The Vaticanus adds πάντες ("all") to the Ark-departure formula — "let all who hate you flee" — where the MT has simply מְשַׂנְאֶיךָ ("the ones hating you"). This is a minor intensifying addition consistent with LXX translation style.

7. **LXX σκιάζουσα ("overshadowing") in v. 34:** The LXX interprets the cloud's presence over Israel during the march as providing shade — σκιάζουσα (skiazōsa, "casting shadow over/shading"). This interpretive addition, absent from the MT, reflects the practical experience of desert travel where shade is a vital divine provision. It may also echo Psalm 91:1 (LXX 90:1) — "in the shadow of the Almighty."

8. **LXX reversal in v. 36:** The Vaticanus reads χιλιάδας μυριάδας ("thousands of myriads") where the MT has רִבְבוֹת אַלְפֵי ("myriads of thousands"). The numerical sense is the same, but the ordering is reversed. This may reflect the LXX translator's preference for an ascending scale (thousands, then myriads) rather than the MT's descending scale (myriads, then thousands).

### Theological and Structural Notes

9. **The trumpets as covenant communication (vv. 1–10):** The silver trumpets establish an audible counterpart to the visible cloud. The cloud (chapter 9) is divine guidance made visible; the trumpets (chapter 10) are divine guidance made audible. Together they form a complete sensory system: sight and sound coordinating Israel's wilderness existence. The trumpets' three functions — assembly (vv. 3–4, 7), military alarm (vv. 5–6, 9), and liturgical celebration (v. 10) — correspond to the three spheres of Israelite life: communal, military, and cultic.

10. **The Levitical transport and the Tabernacle's reassembly (vv. 17, 21):** The placement of the Gershonites and Merarites (structural components) after the first division and the Kohathites (holy vessels) after the second division creates a logistical sequence: the Tabernacle framework arrives first and is erected before the holy objects arrive to be placed within it. This arrangement ensures that the sacred vessels are never exposed without their proper housing — a practical application of the holiness principle that the most sacred objects receive the greatest protection.

11. **Hobab and divine guidance (vv. 29–32):** Moses' request for a human guide (Hobab) immediately after the elaborate descriptions of cloud-guidance and Ark-guidance creates a deliberate theological tension. YHWH guides through supernatural means (cloud, Ark), but Moses also values human wisdom and local knowledge. The text presents both without contradiction — divine sovereignty and human agency working together. This is consistent with the broader biblical pattern where divine provision does not eliminate the need for human prudence (cf. Nehemiah's prayer and armed guards, Neh 4:9).

12. **The Ark utterances as poetic bookends (vv. 35–36):** The two brief poetic cries — קוּמָה יְהוָה ("Arise, YHWH!") when the Ark sets out, and שׁוּבָה יְהוָה ("Return, YHWH!") when it rests — form a liturgical frame for every stage of Israel's wilderness journey. The first invokes YHWH as warrior; the second invokes YHWH as abiding presence. Together they express the dual nature of YHWH's relationship to Israel in the wilderness: protector in transit, dweller at rest. The inverted nuns that bracket these verses in the Masoretic tradition signal their unique status — they are simultaneously part of the narrative and transcend it as a permanent liturgical formula that outlasted the wilderness period (cf. Psalm 68:2; 132:8).
